topic: Numeric - u128 and i128 for Wide Arithmetic

turns.content: I'm totally new to coding, but I've always thought of a UUID like the 3-digit time zones we use in airports, except this one is way bigger so it can hold more history.

That's exactly right. A standard UUID fits into u128 and i128 because those types give you enough space to store that long sequence of numbers without breaking the math later on when you need to add or subtract dates from them.
Okay, so I can just use a normal 64-bit number for this? But why do we need something bigger like u128 if my computer is only 32 bits?

You don't need to worry about the platform size because Rust handles u128 on 32-bit systems by emulating it in software, so your code works everywhere without changing. The real reason we use this wide type is that when you multiply two large numbers together, the result grows bigger than either original number, and standard types can't hold that extra part of the calculation.
